text: Although Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been very effective for single-label image classification, multi-label image classification is still a big challenge. One of the most compelling reasons is the complexity of different possible object layouts and lack of the sufficient amount of training data in most multi-label image classification benchmarks. In this paper, we propose Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP) architecture, a new deep learning approach to accomplish multi-label image classification. Instead of having a fixed number of bounding box regions as in typical CNN, an arbitrary number of object segment hypotheses are input to a shared CNN. Their corresponding outputs are then given to max-pooling operation, in order to obtain the final multi-label classification results. Our network, HCP, is showed to be able to learn the multi-label image classification task from the images and their segment hypotheses without relying to the ground-truth bounding box locations. It is also robust against the noisy or redundant segment hypotheses in an image. The shared CNN part in our network is also easy to be optimized and can fully take the advantage of the pre-trained ImageNet model. We conduct experimental comparisons on two popular benchmarks – the Pascal VOC 2007 and VOC 2012. Our experimental results show that our proposed deep neural network, HCP, outperforms the state-of-the-arts on these two benchmarks; by achieving 90.5% mean Average Precision (mAP) on VOC 2012 while improves to 93.2% when we combine our proposed network, HCP, with the global descriptors of hand-crafted features.
